annotations.label: Fourier transform (integral definition)

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform of a Lebesgue integrable complex-valued function

annotations.note: `Real.fourierIntegral` (specialization of `VectorFourier.fourierIntegral` with the standard additive character `𝐞`) defines the Fourier integral exactly as in the article.

annotations.label: Fourier inversion theorem

anchor.snippet: the corresponding inversion formula for functions satisfying sufficient regularity and decay properties is given by the Fourier inversion theorem

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Integrable.fourierInv_fourier_eq` and `Continuous.fourierInv_fourier_eq` give the Fourier inversion formula on finite-dimensional real inner product spaces.

annotations.label: Fourier transform of the delta function

anchor.snippet: the Fourier transform of the delta function is the constant function

annotations.note: `TemperedDistribution.fourier_delta_zero` states `𝓕 (delta 0) = volume.toTemperedDistribution`, i.e. the Fourier transform of δ is the constant 1 distribution.

anchors.snippet: the Fourier transform of the delta function is the constant function

annotations.label: Angular-frequency convention

anchor.snippet: produces this convention

annotations.note: Mathlib's Fourier transform uses the ordinary-frequency (2π) convention via `fourierChar`; no separate angular-frequency variant `𝓕(f)(ω) = ∫ f(x) e^{-iωx} dx` is defined.

annotations.label: Lebesgue integrable function

anchor.snippet: is called (Lebesgue) integrable if the Lebesgue integral of its absolute value is finite

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Integrable` defines integrability of `f` w.r.t. a measure via `HasFiniteIntegral` (finite integral of `‖f‖`).

anchors.snippet: is called (Lebesgue) integrable if the Lebesgue integral of its absolute value is finite

annotations.label: L^p space

anchor.snippet: is the space of measurable functions for which the norm

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Lp E p μ` (and `MeasureTheory.MemLp`) define the L^p spaces of measurable functions with finite p-seminorm.

annotations.label: Fourier transform is a Banach-algebra homomorphism

anchor.snippet: Fourier transform is that it is a homomorphism of Banach algebras

annotations.note: The ingredients exist — linearity (`fourier_add`/`fourier_smul`), L¹-norm bound (`norm_fourierIntegral_le_integral_norm`), and convolution-to-multiplication (`Real.fourier_mul_convolution_eq`) — but Mathlib does not package the L¹ Fourier transform as a Banach-algebra homomorphism.

annotations.label: Symmetry of FT for real-valued functions

anchor.snippet: has the symmetry property

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named lemma `𝓕(f)(-ξ) = conj(𝓕(f)(ξ))` for real-valued `f` (Hermitian symmetry); grep finds no such conjugation/negation symmetry result for the Fourier integral.

annotations.label: Fourier transform of a periodic function

anchor.snippet: the Fourier transform is a Dirac comb function whose teeth are multiplied by the Fourier series coefficients

annotations.note: No `DiracComb` or `fourier_periodic` identification is present in Mathlib relating Fourier coefficients to a comb-supported tempered distribution.

anchors.snippet: the Fourier transform is a Dirac comb function whose teeth are multiplied by the Fourier series coefficients

annotations.label: Poisson summation formula for sampled FT

anchor.snippet: These samples can be deduced from one cycle of a periodic function

annotations.note: Classical Poisson summation `∑ f(n) = ∑ 𝓕(f)(n)` is in `Real.tsum_eq_tsum_fourier`, which is the underlying identity but not phrased in terms of FT samples vs. periodization.

anchors.snippet: These samples can be deduced from one cycle of a periodic function

annotations.label: Time-reversal property

anchor.snippet: leads to the time-reversal property

annotations.note: `Real.fourierInv_eq_fourier_comp_neg` (and `fourierInv_eq_fourier_neg`) encode `𝓕⁻¹ f (w) = 𝓕 f (-w)`, equivalent to the time-reversal property; no separate `𝓕(f∘neg) = 𝓕(f)∘neg` lemma was found.

anchors.snippet: leads to the time-reversal property

annotations.label: Even/odd symmetry decomposition of FT

anchor.snippet: there is a one-to-one mapping between the four components of a complex time function and the four components of its complex frequency transform

annotations.note: No lemmas in Mathlib decompose the Fourier transform along the even/odd × real/imaginary parts.

annotations.label: FT of a real function is conjugate symmetric (Hermitian)

anchor.snippet: is conjugate symmetric ( a.k.a. Hermitian function )

annotations.note: No `fourierIntegral_conj` / Hermitian-symmetry lemma for real-valued inputs exists in `Mathlib/Analysis/Fourier`.

anchors.snippet: is conjugate symmetric ( a.k.a. Hermitian function )

annotations.label: Zero-frequency component equals total mass / DC bias

anchor.snippet: is the total mass or DC bias of the function

annotations.note: Although the statement is immediate from `fourierIntegral_def` (since `𝐞(0)=1`), there is no dedicated `fourierIntegral_zero_eq_integral` lemma in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform continuity of FT of integrable function

anchor.section: Uniform continuity and the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma
anchor.snippet: of any integrable function

annotations.note: `VectorFourier.fourierIntegral_continuous` proves continuity of `𝓕 f` for integrable `f`, but uniform continuity is not separately established.

anchors.section: Uniform continuity and the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma
anchors.snippet: of any integrable function

annotations.label: Riemann–Lebesgue lemma

anchor.section: Uniform continuity and the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma
anchor.snippet: By the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma

annotations.note: `Real.zero_at_infty_fourier` and `Real.zero_at_infty_vector_fourierIntegral` state that `𝓕 f → 0` at cocompact infinity for `f ∈ L¹`.

anchors.section: Uniform continuity and the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma
anchors.snippet: By the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma

annotations.label: Injectivity of FT on L^1(R)

anchor.section: Uniform continuity and the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma
anchor.snippet: the Fourier transform is injective on L 1 ( R )

annotations.note: No dedicated `Real.fourierIntegral_injective` lemma was found; injectivity is only implicit in inversion-type results requiring extra hypotheses.

anchors.section: Uniform continuity and the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Lp.fourierTransformₗᵢ` packages the Fourier transform on L² as a `LinearIsometryEquiv` (Plancherel), with the norm identity proved as `Lp.norm_fourier_eq`.

annotations.label: Convolution theorem

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform translates between convolution and multiplication of functions

annotations.note: `Real.fourier_mul_convolution_eq` (and the bilinear variant `fourier_bilin_convolution_eq`) prove `𝓕 (f ⋆ g) = 𝓕 f · 𝓕 g`.

annotations.label: Cross-correlation theorem

anchor.snippet: is the cross-correlation of

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated cross-correlation operator nor a `𝓕(f ⋆⋆ g) = conj(𝓕 f) · 𝓕 g` lemma.

annotations.label: FT of a derivative

anchor.snippet: Then the Fourier transform of the derivative is given by

annotations.note: `VectorFourier.fourierIntegral_fderiv` and `fourierIntegral_iteratedFDeriv` give the formula `𝓕(f') = 2πiξ · 𝓕 f` in the general vector-valued setting.

anchors.snippet: Then the Fourier transform of the derivative is given by

annotations.label: Eigenfunctions of the Fourier transform

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform is a linear transform that has eigenfunctions

annotations.note: Mathlib proves that the Gaussian is fixed by the Fourier transform (`fourier_gaussian_pi`, `fourier_gaussian_innerProductSpace`) but does not develop a general theory of FT eigenfunctions.

annotations.label: Hermite functions form an orthonormal eigenbasis for FT

anchor.snippet: the Hermite functions form a complete orthonormal system of eigenfunctions

annotations.note: Hermite polynomials exist in `Mathlib/RingTheory/Polynomial/Hermite`, but the Hermite functions as a complete orthonormal eigenbasis of the Fourier transform on L² are not formalized.

annotations.label: Four-periodicity / Fourier inversion theorem

anchor.snippet: the various forms of the Fourier inversion theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Fourier inversion identity, but no explicit `𝓕^4 = id` four-periodicity statement on a suitable subspace.

anchors.snippet: the various forms of the Fourier inversion theorem

annotations.label: Stone–von Neumann realization of FT as intertwiner

anchor.snippet: According to the Stone–von Neumann theorem

annotations.note: Neither the Heisenberg group nor the Stone–von Neumann theorem appear in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: According to the Stone–von Neumann theorem

annotations.label: Paley–Wiener theorem

anchor.snippet: The Paley–Wiener theorem says that f is smooth

annotations.note: Grep for `Paley.?Wiener` / `PaleyWiener` returns no matches in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The Paley–Wiener theorem says that f is smooth

annotations.label: Relation between FT and Laplace transform

anchor.snippet: is related to the Laplace transform

annotations.note: The Laplace transform is not defined in Mathlib (no `Laplace.?[Tt]ransform` matches), so a relation to it cannot be stated.

annotations.label: Complex-line inversion for causal exponentially-bounded functions

anchor.snippet: Theorem: If f ( t ) = 0 for t < 0

annotations.note: No Bromwich/complex-line inversion result for causal functions is present, since the Laplace transform itself is absent from Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Theorem: If f ( t ) = 0 for t < 0

annotations.label: Mellin inversion formula for the Laplace transform

anchor.snippet: This theorem implies the Mellin inversion formula for the Laplace transformation

annotations.note: Mathlib has `mellin_inversion` (`mellinInv_mellin_eq`) for the Mellin transform itself, but no derivation as an inversion formula for the (absent) Laplace transform.

anchors.snippet: This theorem implies the Mellin inversion formula for the Laplace transformation

annotations.label: Fourier transform on Euclidean space R^n

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform can be defined in any arbitrary number of dimensions

annotations.note: `VectorFourier.fourierIntegral` defines the Fourier integral on any finite-dimensional real vector space (in particular on Rⁿ via the dot product).

anchors.snippet: The Fourier transform can be defined in any arbitrary number of dimensions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Bochner-style Fourier-transform-of-radial-functions / spherical-harmonics decomposition.

anchor.snippet: This is essentially the Hankel transform

annotations.note: Grep for `Hankel` finds no Mathlib files; no Bessel-kernel/Hankel transform is defined.

annotations.note: No Tomas–Stein or general Fourier restriction theorem appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extension of FT to L^2

anchor.snippet: The definition of the Fourier transform naturally extends from

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Lp.fourierTransformₗᵢ` defines the Fourier transform on `Lp E F 2` by isometric extension from the Schwartz space.

annotations.label: FT on L^2 is unitary

anchor.snippet: is a unitary operator

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Lp.fourierTransformₗᵢ` is a `LinearIsometryEquiv`, which on a Hilbert space is exactly a unitary operator; `Lp.norm_fourier_eq` records norm preservation.

anchors.snippet: is a unitary operator

annotations.label: Hausdorff–Young inequality

anchor.snippet: by the Hausdorff–Young inequality

annotations.note: Grep for `Hausdorff.?Young` returns no Mathlib matches.

annotations.label: Fourier transform of a tempered distribution

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform can also be defined for tempered distributions

annotations.note: The Fourier transform on `𝓢'(E, F)` is defined via `𝓕 f g = f (𝓕 g)` (see `fourier_apply`), and an instance is registered making `𝓕` available on tempered distributions.

anchors.snippet: The Fourier transform can also be defined for tempered distributions

annotations.label: Fourier–Stieltjes transform of a finite Borel measure

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform of a finite Borel measure

annotations.note: `charFun μ t = ∫ x, exp(⟪x,t⟫ * I) ∂μ` is the characteristic function / Fourier–Stieltjes transform of a finite measure (also `charFun_eq_fourierIntegral` connects it explicitly to `𝓕`).

anchors.snippet: The Fourier transform of a finite Borel measure

annotations.label: Bochner's theorem

anchor.snippet: Bochner's theorem characterizes which functions may arise

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Bochner` references are all to Bochner integration, not to Bochner's theorem characterizing positive-definite Fourier–Stieltjes transforms.

annotations.label: Fourier transform on a locally compact abelian group

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform may be generalized to any locally compact abelian group

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `PontryaginDual A` (continuous homs `A →* Circle`) but does not develop the Fourier transform `L¹(G) → C₀(Ĝ)` for general LCA groups; only the Euclidean and finite-abelian cases are formalized.

anchors.snippet: The Fourier transform may be generalized to any locally compact abelian group

annotations.label: FT as a special case of the Gelfand transform

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform is also a special case of the Gelfand transform

annotations.note: `GelfandDuality` exists in Mathlib but no result identifies the Fourier transform with the Gelfand transform of `L¹(G)`.

annotations.label: Fourier transform on a compact non-abelian group

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform can also be defined for functions on a non-abelian group

annotations.note: No operator-valued / non-abelian Fourier transform via irreducible unitary representations is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Grep for `Peter.?Weyl` / `PeterWeyl` returns no matches in Mathlib.
